title: Request Latency Measurement And Warm-Path Isolation Tasklist
mainGoal: Measure warm-only repeated requests against one route, separate static, redirect, controller, and session routes, isolate cold tenant spawn cost from steady-state request cost, and generate one dedicated performance report focused only on request latency under the current single-main-process PM2 fork configuration.

tasklistPolicy.orderingRule: Ordered by latency-analysis dependency: first lock the runtime topology and measurement rules, then collect cold and warm data separately by route type, then compare those measurements, and finally publish one dedicated latency report.
tasklistPolicy.focusRule: This tasklist is not a general installer lifecycle rerun. It is a narrow request-latency measurement pass centered on route timing quality and on eliminating mixed cold-path and warm-path numbers from the same summary.
tasklistPolicy.readerNote: The active baseline for this tasklist is the March 23, 2026 follow-up run plus the user's change to `ecosystem.config.js` so PM2 now runs one `fork` instance instead of clustered mains. All new latency measurements should respect that single-main-process assumption.

sourceReports.topic: Latest successful installer, smoke, uninstall, and purge run with route timing samples
Latest completed tasklist that corrected the runtime permission blockers
Current PM2 runtime shape with `exec_mode: "fork"` and `instances: 1`
Current smoke route set for redirect, static asset, controller, and session routes

taskGroups.title: Lock The Measurement Baseline
taskGroups.goal: Make the latency pass reproducible by fixing the runtime topology, host shape, and timing rules before collecting any numbers.
pointsOfAttention: The user has already changed PM2 to `fork` with one instance because main must be single-process. The latency pass should treat that as the active baseline, not as an optional variation.
The local curl rule should stay strict: if a localhost request takes more than 1 second to return, it is already a timeout for this measurement set.
The tenant host and runtime state should be reset deliberately so cold-path and warm-path measurements are not accidentally mixed.

tasks.title: Confirm the single-main-process runtime shape
tasks.reportedGoal: Ensure the latency run reflects the new PM2 topology instead of the older clustered behavior.

subtasks.title: Verify `app/ecosystem.config.js` still uses `exec_mode: "fork"` and `instances: 1` at run time
Record that the request-latency run is intentionally single-main-process and should not be compared directly to earlier clustered samples without calling out the topology change
Capture whether the target run uses the same scaffold tenant and host headers as the latest successful permission-policy report

tasks.title: Define the timing protocol
tasks.reportedGoal: Set one timing method for cold and warm measurements so the results are comparable and auditable.

subtasks.title: Use the same local transport and host-header shape for every route, such as `https://127.0.0.1` with `Host: fakedomain.com`
Use `curl --max-time 1` for every local request and record the actual `time_total` for successful responses
Define one cold-run procedure and one warm-run procedure so the first request after tenant spawn is never mixed into the repeated warm sample set

taskGroups.title: Measure Cold Path And Tenant Spawn Cost
taskGroups.goal: Capture the one-time latency cost of the first request that must trigger tenant process creation and route-path setup.
pointsOfAttention: Cold-path measurement should begin from a known state where the runtime is up but the target tenant process is not yet warm.
The first route chosen for cold measurement should be explicit, because static and controller routes may trigger different work.
This group should isolate tenant spawn and first-hit overhead instead of averaging it together with later steady-state responses.

tasks.title: Measure the first successful request after runtime start
tasks.reportedGoal: Record the latency of the earliest successful route that proves the runtime is serving the scaffold tenant.

subtasks.title: Start the runtime in the single-instance fork configuration and time the first successful request for one chosen route
Record whether that first successful request is a redirect, static asset, controller, or session route
Capture PM2 or runtime logs that show whether tenant spawn happened on that first-hit path

tasks.title: Isolate explicit tenant controller cold-start cost
tasks.reportedGoal: Measure the additional latency of the first controller-backed request that must talk to a tenant process.

subtasks.title: Choose one controller route such as `/hello` as the canonical cold controller measurement
Measure the first controller request after a fresh runtime start or after explicitly clearing the relevant warm state
Record whether the measured latency is dominated by tenant spawn, route resolution, or session work by correlating it with runtime logs

taskGroups.title: Measure Warm-Only Repeated Requests
taskGroups.goal: Collect clean steady-state latency numbers after the runtime and target tenant route are already warm.
pointsOfAttention: Warm-only means the tenant process, route cache, and session path assumptions are already established before the repeated sample set begins.
One-off spikes should still be recorded, but they should not be mislabeled as cold spawn if the tenant is already warm.
Use enough repetitions to see stability, not only one best-case and one worst-case sample.

tasks.title: Measure one warm-only repeated route set
tasks.reportedGoal: Produce a repeated sample set for one route after it is already warm.

subtasks.title: Choose one representative route and explicitly warm it first before timing repeated requests
Run a repeated local curl series with `--max-time 1` and record every successful `time_total` value
Summarize best, median, worst, and spread for the warm-only repeated route

tasks.title: Repeat the warm-only method across route classes
tasks.reportedGoal: Gather comparable steady-state latency samples for redirect, static, controller, and session routes.

subtasks.title: Warm and measure `/` as the redirect route
Warm and measure `/htm/index.htm` or `/htm/cached.htm` as the static route
Warm and measure `/hello` as the controller route and `/session` as the session route

taskGroups.title: Separate Route Types And Explain The Gaps
taskGroups.goal: Turn the collected route timings into a route-class comparison that explains why redirect, static, controller, and session paths differ.
pointsOfAttention: The route-class table should make cold and warm numbers visibly separate.
Do not compare a first-hit controller route directly against a warm static asset and call that one latency ranking.
The explanation should connect the numbers back to the real request path, not only repeat the measurements.

tasks.title: Build a cold-versus-warm comparison table
tasks.reportedGoal: Show the route timings in a format that separates first-hit cost from steady-state latency.

subtasks.title: List one cold sample and one warm summary for each measured route class
Call out which route classes stay consistently low and which classes still show material warm-path spread
Flag any warm-path result that still looks suspiciously high for localhost even after single-instance fork mode and warmup

tasks.title: Explain the steady-state overhead sources
tasks.reportedGoal: Map the observed route gaps back to concrete parts of the runtime path.

subtasks.title: Explain the difference between redirect/static handling and controller/session handling in the request pipeline
Call out any remaining fixed costs from HTTPS, manager RPC, queue coordination, tenant RPC, or session handling that still affect warm requests
Decide whether the remaining warm-path latency is acceptable for localhost measurement or should become a new optimization task

taskGroups.title: Publish The Dedicated Request-Latency Report
taskGroups.goal: Write one `report/performance` artifact that is about request latency only, not the full installer lifecycle.
pointsOfAttention: This report should be narrower than the earlier lifecycle reports and should not bury the latency table inside unrelated install and cleanup details.
The report must state the runtime shape explicitly: single `fork` instance, strict 1 second local timeout, chosen host header, and warmup method.
If the results still show a large warm-path spread, the report should name the likely source and recommend the next measurement or optimization step.

tasks.title: Write the latency-focused performance report
tasks.reportedGoal: Create a dedicated request-latency report under `report/performance`.

subtasks.title: Record environment, runtime topology, host header, timeout rule, and measurement procedure
Include cold-path timing, warm-only repeated timing, and route-class comparison results
Summarize whether the local warm-path latency looks acceptable under the single-instance fork model

tasks.title: Turn the latency findings into the next action
tasks.reportedGoal: Make the latency report immediately actionable.

subtasks.title: If the warm-path numbers look healthy, mark the previous mixed cold/warm interpretation as resolved
If the warm-path spread still looks too large, create one follow-up tasklist focused on the specific layer that appears to dominate latency

executionGateForRequestLatencyMeasuremen.description: This latency tasklist is complete only when one dedicated measurement pass has been run on the current single-instance fork runtime, cold tenant spawn cost is separated from warm-only route timings, redirect/static/controller/session routes are compared explicitly, every local curl used a 1 second timeout cap, and one new performance report records the latency-focused outcome.
requiredChecks: The active PM2 runtime shape is verified as `fork` with `instances: 1`
The local timing protocol uses `curl --max-time 1` consistently
Cold tenant spawn timing is measured separately from warm repeated timing
Redirect, static, controller, and session routes each have their own latency samples
At least one warm-only repeated route sample set is summarized with spread information
A dedicated `report/performance` artifact is written for request latency only
